title: Allow SAW to symbolically execute header-only system templates (STL gap, part 1 of 2)
description:
## Problem

Every test in `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/` is `Expected = 'DISPROVED'` for algebraically correct code, because saw-spec-gen blanket-blacklists any callee whose declaration lives in a system include path. `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/README.md` documents the behaviour. The trigger:

- `src/gen_verify.rs:186-196` — stops walking the callgraph at `callee.is_system`, even when `callee.has_body == true`.
- `src/gen_verify.rs:210-213` — then treats any bodied-but-system callee as external and emits an adversarial override (return value unconstrained).

The blanket rule was added to keep `printf`/`memcpy`/CRT functions out of the symbolic-execution path (they use intrinsics SAW can't lower). It sweeps up **header-only STL templates that have full bitcode bodies after clang instantiates them** — `std::max`, `std::min`, `std::clamp`, `std::pair::first`, `std::tuple::get`, `std::get<I>`, etc. Those bodies are pure arithmetic / pointer math and Crucible handles them natively.

## Proposal

Replace the binary `is_system` gate with a two-step gate:

1. **Has a body?** (already checked.)
2. **Is the body "Crucible-safe"?** — uses only opcodes Crucible can lower without an external override. If yes, recurse; if no, fall back to the adversarial override.

### Crucible-safe opcode allow-list (initial draft)

Allowed:
- All arithmetic / bit / comparison ops: `add`, `sub`, `mul`, `udiv`, `sdiv`, `urem`, `srem`, `shl`, `lshr`, `ashr`, `and`, `or`, `xor`, `icmp`, `fcmp`, `select`, `phi`.
- Control flow: `br` (cond + uncond), `switch`, `ret`.
- Memory: `alloca`, `load`, `store`, `getelementptr`, `bitcast`, `addrspacecast`, `inttoptr`, `ptrtoint`, `extractvalue`, `insertvalue`, `extractelement`, `insertelement`.
- Pure intrinsics: `llvm.fabs.*`, `llvm.sqrt.*`, `llvm.abs.*`, `llvm.umin.*`, `llvm.umax.*`, `llvm.smin.*`, `llvm.smax.*`, `llvm.bswap.*`, `llvm.ctlz.*`, `llvm.cttz.*`, `llvm.ctpop.*`, `llvm.assume`, `llvm.expect.*`, `llvm.lifetime.*` (no-op), `llvm.memcpy.*` / `llvm.memmove.*` / `llvm.memset.*` (Crucible already models these).
- `call` to any function that itself passes the safety check (recurse transitively, with a visited-set to break cycles).

Disallowed (treat as external instead):
- `invoke` / `landingpad` / `resume` / `catchpad` / `catchswitch` / `cleanuppad` (EH path — already handled by the exception-lower pass when enabled, but the safety check should still bail here).
- `atomicrmw`, `cmpxchg`, `fence` (Crucible's concurrency story is incomplete).
- Inline `asm`.
- `call` to a function that is **not** safe (recurse).
- `va_arg` / `va_start` / `va_end`.
- Any `llvm.*` intrinsic not on the allow-list above.

### Implementation plan

1. Add `src/transform/crucible_safety.rs` (~150 lines, well under the 500 limit) with one entry point:

   ```rust
   pub struct SafetyReport {
       pub safe: bool,
       pub first_unsafe_inst: Option<String>,  // for diagnostics
   }
   pub fn is_crucible_safe(
       fn_name: &str,
       ir_module: &llvm_ir::Module,
       visited: &mut HashSet<String>,
   ) -> SafetyReport;
   ```

   - Uses `llvm-ir` crate (already a dep — see `src/parsers/llvm_ir.rs`).
   - Walks every instruction in every basic block.
   - For `call`/`invoke`, looks up the callee in the same module and recurses (with the visited-set to break cycles; a recursive function is unsafe by definition because saw-spec-gen can't terminate symbolic execution there).
   - Returns the first instruction that violated the allow-list for stderr diagnostics (`SAW_SPEC_GEN_DEBUG=1` prints these).

2. In `src/gen_verify.rs:186-213`, replace:

   ```rust
   if callee.has_body && !callee.is_system {
       worklist.push(callee);
   }
   ```

   with:

   ```rust
   if callee.has_body && (
        !callee.is_system
        || is_crucible_safe(&callee.name, ir_module, &mut visited).safe
   ) {
       worklist.push(callee);
   }
   ```

   And mirror the change in the external-call filter at L210-L213 (a callee with a body that passes the safety check is **not** external).

3. Cache safety results per module — `Lazy<HashMap<String, SafetyReport>>` — because the same `std::max<u32>` symbol is queried once per caller.

4. Wire a kill-switch env var `SAW_SPEC_GEN_NO_SYSTEM_RECURSION=1` that forces the old behaviour, for bisecting regressions.

### Acceptance

- [ ] New `src/transform/crucible_safety.rs` with unit tests for: pure-arithmetic body (safe), body with `invoke` (unsafe), body with `atomicrmw` (unsafe), body with `call @printf` (unsafe via transitive check), recursive body (unsafe).
- [ ] The following STL coverage cases flip to `Expected = 'VERIFIED'` and pass:
  - `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/algorithm_max/`
  - `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/algorithm_min/`
  - `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/algorithm_clamp/`
  - `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/pair_first/`
  - `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/tuple_get/`
- [ ] Rename the flipped cases from `add_one_gap_disproved.cpp` back to `add_one_verified.cpp` (suffix matches the new expected verdict) and update `tests/e2e/cases.psd1`.
- [ ] Update `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/README.md` to mark which cases are now verified and which remain `DISPROVED` (vector/unique_ptr/accumulate — those have allocator-state issues that this change does not address; track them under the part-2 issue).
- [ ] Full e2e suite stays green (`pwsh tests/e2e/Run-E2ETests.ps1`). No regressions on `01-tutorial`, `02-havoc-coverage`, `06-throws-exception`, `08-overrides`.
- [ ] `SAW_SPEC_GEN_NO_SYSTEM_RECURSION=1` reproduces the old verdicts.
- [ ] `src/gen_verify.rs` does not exceed 500 non-whitespace lines (it is at ~480 today per repo memory; if the change pushes over, extract the worklist into a helper module first).

## Out of scope (track separately)

- `std::vector`, `std::unique_ptr`, `std::accumulate`, `std::string` — these involve allocator state and iterator pairs that defeat the compositional havoc model even with symbolic execution enabled. They need either custom overrides (part 2: see follow-up issue for "built-in STL override library") or a richer heap model.
- Symbol-name allow-list as a fallback — could be added later if the safety check proves too coarse, but start with the principled approach.

title: Fix compiler warnings and add unit tests for constraint derivation
description: 14 compiler warnings from unused enum variants, unused fields, etc. Fix by either wiring them through the pipeline or gating with #[allow] where they are genuinely for future use. Add unit tests for derive_constraints: test that readonly params get AllocReadonly, that Bool returns get no extra constraint, that Enum types get discriminant range preconditions.

title: CI: GitHub Actions workflow for build + test
description: Set up GitHub Actions workflow that runs on PR and push to main. Steps: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy, cargo test, cargo build --release. Test on windows-latest and ubuntu-latest.

title: Add integration test with MSP Rust request_validator
description: End-to-end test: use mir-json output from the Rust MSP crate, feed to saw-spec-gen from-mir-json, verify generated specs have correct immutable reference constraints, correct Result/Option return type discriminant bounds, and that specs can be loaded by SAW.

title: Add integration test with MSP C++ RequestValidator
description: End-to-end test: run clang -ast-dump=json on the MSP C++ RequestValidator.cpp, feed to saw-spec-gen, verify the generated .saw specs have correct readonly annotations for const& params, correct return type (bool), and that the specs can be loaded by SAW without errors. Add test fixtures in tests/ directory.

title: Emit mir_verify specs in addition to llvm_verify specs
description: saw_emit.rs currently only generates llvm_verify-style SAW scripts (llvm_alloc, llvm_fresh_var, etc.). Add a parallel emitter for mir_verify specs using mir_alloc, mir_fresh_var, mir_points_to, mir_enum_value, mir_precond. The constraint derivation is the same — only the emitted SAW commands differ. Add a --target flag (llvm or mir) to the CLI.

title: MIR JSON: resolve struct/ADT layouts from mir-json type definitions
description: mir-json output includes ADT (algebraic data type) definitions with field names, types, and layout info (size, alignment, field offsets). Parse these to generate correct SAW type allocations. Currently MIR types are returned as Opaque. Need to map ty::Adt::HASH references to their definitions and emit llvm_struct_type or mir_adt with correct fields.

title: MIR JSON: parse enum variants and generate discriminant constraints
description: mir-json linked-mir.json contains full enum definitions with variant names, discriminant values, and field types. Parse these to generate llvm_precond/mir_precond constraints that restrict discriminants to valid values. Covers Option (0=None, 1=Some), Result (0=Ok, 1=Err), and custom enums like LatchableKey, KeyOperationError, MspState. Currently only handles primitive types.

title: Clang AST: parse SAL annotations from MSVC source
description: SAL annotations (_In_, _Out_, _Inout_, _In_reads_(n), _Out_writes_(n), _Ret_maybenull_, _Must_inspect_result_) are not in LLVM IR. clang -ast-dump exposes them as AnnotateAttr nodes when using clang's SAL emulation headers. Current parse_sal_annotation handles basic cases but needs testing with real MSVC SAL headers and edge cases like _In_opt_, _Inout_updates_bytes_, _Post_satisfies_.

title: Clang AST: handle class/struct field types and sizes
description: parse_cpp_type currently returns Opaque for any non-primitive type. Need to resolve class/struct types by walking the AST to find RecordDecl nodes, extracting field names, types, and byte offsets. This enables generating llvm_alias or llvm_struct_type in SAW specs with correct sizes. Key for MSVC std::string (SSO layout), HeaderMap, Request, etc.

title: Auto-promote guess_length_companion result into a real _In_reads_ allocation (behind --auto-buffer-length flag)
description:
## Problem

`src/constraints/derive.rs:377-406` (`guess_length_companion`) already implements a name-based heuristic that pairs a pointer parameter with a likely sibling length parameter (`buf`↔`{n,len,count,size,length,num,nbuf,cbBuf,cchBuf,len_buf,...}`). When the heuristic **fires**, the result is only emitted as a TODO comment (`derive.rs:166-187`) instructing the user to add `_In_reads_(N)` to the C++ declaration or hand-edit the generated `.saw`. The found companion's name is told to the user but never used.

The auto-spec then allocates a **single element** for the buffer — almost always wrong, and the only signal is a comment block buried in the generated `.saw`.

## Proposal

Add an opt-in CLI flag that promotes a high-confidence companion match into a real `_In_reads_` / `_Out_writes_`-equivalent SAW allocation: a sized `llvm_array` plus a `llvm_precond { companion <= DEFAULT_PARAMREF_MAX_LEN }`. The existing explicit-`_In_reads_(pname)` code path at `derive.rs:227-247` already does exactly this; this issue extends the same machinery to inferred pairings.

### CLI surface

```
--auto-buffer-length             (default off)
--auto-buffer-length=strict      (current default; only fire when sibling is a single unambiguous match)
--auto-buffer-length=permissive  (fire on any match, even ambiguous)
```

Off by default to avoid silently changing existing specs.

### Confidence gate — when to promote

Promote only if **all** of:

1. Sibling parameter exists at the same call site (already produced by `guess_length_companion`).
2. Sibling type is an integral type (`UnsignedInt(N)` or `SignedInt(N)` with `N >= 8`). Reject `Float`/`Pointer`/`Bool`/`Enum`.
3. Sibling is not itself a pointer companion of a different parameter in the same function. (Avoid `void copy(void *src, void *dst, size_t n)` from binding `n` to both `src` and `dst`. Bind to neither, emit the TODO as today.)
4. Pointer parameter's pointee size is **known** (`TypeInfo::UnsignedInt(_) | SignedInt(_) | Bool | ByteArray(_)`). If the pointee is a struct with unknown layout, fall back to TODO — the array stride is ambiguous.
5. In `strict` mode: exactly one candidate sibling. In `permissive` mode: first match wins (current behaviour of `guess_length_companion`).

When all five hold:
- Replace `(llvm_int 8)` allocation with `(llvm_array <companion> (llvm_int <pointee-bits>))`.
- Push `llvm_precond {{ ({companion} : [64]) <= {DEFAULT_PARAMREF_MAX_LEN} }};` (mirror of `derive.rs:233-238`).
- Replace the TODO comment block with a single one-line `// auto-inferred: {ptr_name} sized by sibling parameter '{companion}' (use --auto-buffer-length=off to disable)`.

When the gate fails, emit the existing TODO block but include the rejection reason in it: `//   Heuristic gated off because {reason}.`

### Implementation plan

1. Add `--auto-buffer-length` (default `"off"`, with `"strict"` and `"permissive"` as accepted values) to `gen-verify` in `src/main.rs`. Mirror onto `gen-verify-rust`, `from-clang-ast`, `from-mir-json`, `from-llvm-ir`.

2. Thread the flag value as `BufferLengthMode { Off, Strict, Permissive }` through to `derive_constraints` in `src/constraints/derive.rs`.

3. Extract a new private helper next to `guess_length_companion`:

   ```rust
   struct BufferPromotion {
       companion: String,
       elem_bits: u32,
   }
   fn try_promote_length_companion(
       param: &Param,
       all_params: &[Param],
       inner_ty: &TypeInfo,
       mode: BufferLengthMode,
   ) -> Result<BufferPromotion, GateFailure>;
   ```

   `GateFailure` is an enum carrying the human-readable rejection reason for the TODO block.

4. In the existing `if is_indirect && !void_ptr_as_value && !matches!(alloc_type, FreshVar) && !has_size_annotation` arm (`derive.rs:160-189`), call `try_promote_length_companion`:
   - On `Ok(BufferPromotion { companion, elem_bits })`:
     - Switch `alloc_type` to a new `AllocType::AllocReadonlyArray { len_expr: companion, elem_bits }` (or pass through the existing `AllocType` plumbing if it already supports arrays — check `src/constraints/saw_type.rs`).
     - Push the `<= DEFAULT_PARAMREF_MAX_LEN` precondition.
     - Push the one-line auto-inferred comment.
   - On `Err(reason)`: keep the existing TODO block, append the reason.

5. Update `src/emit/saw_emit/llvm_spec.rs` (and `mir_spec.rs` if Rust shares the path) to emit the array allocation correctly for the new alloc type.

### Tests

Add unit tests in `derive_tests.rs`:

- `auto_buffer_length_off_emits_todo_even_with_match` — flag off, sibling exists, expect TODO unchanged.
- `auto_buffer_length_strict_promotes_unambiguous_pair` — `(uint8_t *buf, size_t len)` → array allocation + precond.
- `auto_buffer_length_strict_rejects_ambiguous` — two candidate siblings → TODO with "ambiguous: candidates `len_a`, `len_b`".
- `auto_buffer_length_strict_rejects_float_companion` — sibling is `float n` → TODO with "non-integral sibling type".
- `auto_buffer_length_strict_rejects_shared_companion` — `(void *src, void *dst, size_t n)` → both pointers get TODO with "sibling `n` matches multiple pointers".
- `auto_buffer_length_strict_rejects_unknown_pointee` — `(struct Opaque *p, size_t len)` → TODO with "pointee size unknown".

Add an e2e case under `tests/e2e/cases/05-string-ops/auto_buffer_length/`:
- `process_bytes_verified.cpp` taking `(const uint8_t *buf, size_t len)` and a Cryptol spec that XORs all bytes.
- Without the flag: today's TODO + single-element allocation → DISPROVED.
- With `--auto-buffer-length=strict`: VERIFIED.
- Add two manifest entries to `tests/e2e/cases.psd1`, one per flag setting, so both verdicts are checked.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `--auto-buffer-length=[off|strict|permissive]` flag wired on `gen-verify` and `gen-verify-rust` with `off` as default.
- [ ] When promotion fires, the generated spec contains the sized `llvm_array` allocation and the `<= DEFAULT_PARAMREF_MAX_LEN` precondition (mirror of the explicit-annotation path).
- [ ] When promotion is rejected, the existing TODO block is preserved and augmented with the rejection reason.
- [ ] All six unit tests above pass.
- [ ] New e2e case demonstrates the verdict flip under the flag.
- [ ] Existing e2e tests pass unchanged (flag defaults to off → no behavioural change to specs that don't pass it).
- [ ] `src/constraints/derive.rs` stays under 500 non-whitespace lines (currently large; if the new helper pushes over, extract it into `src/constraints/buffer_length.rs`).

## Out of scope

- Inferring the **bound** from SAL annotations on the sibling itself (e.g., `_In_range_(0, 4096) size_t len`). The current `DEFAULT_PARAMREF_MAX_LEN` is good enough for the first cut; file separately if a real codebase needs tighter bounds.
- Dataflow-based length inference (walking the LLVM IR to find which integer parameter feeds into the GEP indices of which pointer). The name heuristic catches ~90% of Win32 / POSIX-style code; dataflow is a much larger project.

title: Built-in functional overrides for common STL templates (STL gap, part 2 of 2)
description:
## Problem

The part-1 issue (saw-spec-gen-9x5) lets Crucible execute header-only STL templates whose bodies are pure arithmetic. That covers `std::max`, `std::min`, `std::clamp`, `std::pair`/`tuple` getters. It does **not** cover STL functions whose bodies touch allocator state or iterators:

- `std::accumulate(begin, end, init)` — body loops over iterator pairs; safe arithmetic, but the iterator model is heap-state-flavoured and the safety check will reject it (it calls allocator-flavoured helpers).
- `std::vector<T>::push_back` / `back()` — touches `_Mypair._Myval2._Myfirst/_Mylast` allocator-internal pointers.
- `std::unique_ptr<T>::operator*` / `get()` — touches `_Mypair._Myval2` pointer field.
- `std::string::operator[]` / `data()` — short-string-optimisation branch is unfollowable.

For these, even with part-1 enabled, the answer needs to be "use a hand-tuned override that says what the function does semantically", not "let Crucible follow the body".

## Proposal

Ship a small library of built-in functional overrides under `src/emit/saw_emit/stl_overrides/`, one file per template family, registered into a `StlOverrideRegistry` consulted by the external-call filter in `src/gen_verify.rs`.

### Initial scope (8 overrides)

For each, the override should declare the spec in SAW syntax with a Cryptol postcondition that exactly captures the operational semantics, treating the STL type as an opaque struct of the right size.

| Symbol family | Override semantics |
|---|---|
| `std::accumulate<It, T, Op>` | `result == foldl op init [a[0]..a[n-1]]` over the contiguous range view of `[begin, end)`; requires a Cryptol model of `op` (default `+`). |
| `std::vector<T>::push_back(v)` | `size_after == size_before + 1`, `back() == v`, all other elements unchanged, capacity is havoced (allocator decision). |
| `std::vector<T>::back()` | `result == elements[size - 1]`, no mutation. |
| `std::vector<T>::operator[](i)` | `result == elements[i]`, precondition `i < size`. |
| `std::unique_ptr<T>::operator*()` | `result == *pointee`, no mutation. |
| `std::make_unique<T>(args...)` | Allocates, initializes pointee from `args`, returns pointer to a fresh allocation. |
| `std::string::operator[](i)` | `result == bytes[i]`, precondition `i < size`. |
| `std::string::data()` | Returns pointer into a contiguous byte buffer of length `size`. |

### Implementation plan

1. Create `src/emit/saw_emit/stl_overrides/mod.rs` with:

   ```rust
   pub struct StlOverride {
       pub matches: fn(&SymbolInfo) -> bool,  // by mangled-name pattern + signature shape
       pub emit_spec: fn(&FunctionInfo, &mut SpecWriter),
   }
   pub fn registry() -> &'static [StlOverride];
   ```

2. One file per override family — keeps each well under the 500-line limit:
   - `accumulate.rs`, `vector.rs`, `unique_ptr.rs`, `string.rs`, `pair_tuple.rs` (the part-1 fallback for when symexec is disabled).

3. Pattern-match on the **demangled** name (Itanium for clang+libstdc++/libc++, MSVC for clang-cl). Use `cpp_demangle` (likely already a transitive dep; if not, add it). Keep matchers explicit:

   ```rust
   // Matches std::vector<T>::push_back(const T&) and std::vector<T>::push_back(T&&)
   fn matches(s: &SymbolInfo) -> bool {
       let dem = demangle(&s.mangled_name)?;
       dem.starts_with("std::vector<")
           && dem.contains(">::push_back(")
   }
   ```

4. In `src/gen_verify.rs`, before the "treat as external" fallback at L210-L213, consult the registry:

   ```rust
   if let Some(over) = stl_overrides::registry().iter().find(|o| (o.matches)(&sym)) {
       (over.emit_spec)(&fn_info, &mut writer);
       continue;  // skip the adversarial-override path
   }
   ```

5. Provide a kill-switch env var `SAW_SPEC_GEN_NO_STL_OVERRIDES=1`.

### Acceptance

- [ ] `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/numeric_accumulate/` flips to `VERIFIED` (depends on `std::accumulate` override).
- [ ] `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/vector_back_havoc/` flips to `VERIFIED` (depends on `vector::push_back` + `vector::back` overrides composing).
- [ ] `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/unique_ptr_deref_havoc/` flips to `VERIFIED` (depends on `make_unique` + `operator*` overrides composing).
- [ ] Renamed `add_one_gap_disproved.cpp` → `add_one_verified.cpp` for each, manifest updated.
- [ ] New cases under `tests/e2e/cases/10-stl-coverage/` cover at least one **DISPROVED** path for each override (e.g. `accumulate_returns_wrong_sum_disproved.cpp`) so the overrides are not trivially over-permissive.
- [ ] `SAW_SPEC_GEN_NO_STL_OVERRIDES=1` falls back to the part-1 behaviour.
- [ ] Every new file under 500 non-whitespace lines.

## Notes

- libstdc++ on Linux mangles differently from MSVC STL on Windows. The matcher must cover both. Plan for at least two test runs in CI (already the case; this just adds coverage to both).
- Out of scope: `<chrono>`, `<thread>`, `<atomic>`, `<random>` — file separately if/when a real test case appears.

title: Enum constraints over-constrain non-contiguous discriminants (variants.len() heuristic)
Wire --precond / --constraints CLI flag to attach user preconditions to generated specs
description:
## Problem

The xip umbrella (saw-spec-gen-xip) promised two complementary mechanisms for value preconditions. The automatic (type-driven) half shipped. The user-supplied half did not — there is currently no way to attach an arbitrary `llvm_precond` to a generated spec from the CLI, despite source comments referring to a `--precond` flag.

Concrete proof that the flag is missing:
- `src/constraints/derive.rs:201-203` — "Indirect users can attach a precondition via `--precond` until that naming is unified." No such flag.
- `src/constraints/saw_type.rs:73` — error message says "Use --precond or ..." No such flag.
- `src/constraints/derive_tests.rs:141` — comment "Users can attach one via `--precond` if they need it." No such flag.
- `grep -E 'long\s*=\s*"precond' src/main.rs` returns zero matches.

The hand-written MSP `verify_attestation.saw` adds `llvm_precond {{ auth_result <= 2 }}`. There is no way to regenerate that spec from saw-spec-gen today — users hand-edit after each regen, defeating the regen-on-change workflow.

## Proposal

Wire a flag on the `gen-verify`, `gen-verify-rust`, `from-clang-ast`, `from-mir-json`, and `from-llvm-ir` subcommands.

### Surface (preferred)

```
--precond <fn-name>:<cryptol-expr>     (repeatable)
--constraints <path>                    (TOML or JSON file)
```

`--precond` is the quick path; `--constraints` is the persistent path.

#### `--precond` repeated flag

```
saw-spec-gen gen-verify ... \
    --precond 'AttestKeyOwnership:auth_result <= 2' \
    --precond 'AttestKeyOwnership:slot < 64'
```

Each repetition pushes one extra `llvm_precond {{ <cryptol-expr> }};` into the named function's spec.

#### `--constraints constraints.toml`

```toml
[AttestKeyOwnership]
preconds = [
  "auth_result <= 2",
  "slot < 64",
]

[ProcessRequest]
preconds = ["len > 0", "len <= 4096"]
```

The TOML key is the **source function name** (not the mangled symbol). Functions not mentioned in the file get no extra clauses. Unknown function names → warning printed to stderr, exit code unchanged (so a stale constraints file does not break the build).

## Implementation plan

1. Add `#[arg(long = "precond", value_name = "FN:EXPR", action = ArgAction::Append)]` and `#[arg(long = "constraints", value_name = "FILE")]` to `gen-verify` in `src/main.rs`. Mirror onto the other subcommands that emit specs (start with `gen-verify` and `gen-verify-rust`; add the rest if trivial).
2. Parse both into a single `UserPreconds: HashMap<String, Vec<String>>` keyed by source function name.
3. Plumb that map through to `derive_constraints` in `src/constraints/derive.rs`. The user preconds are not per-parameter — they reference free variables that come from any parameter — so they need a new field on `SpecConstraint`:

   ```rust
   pub struct SpecConstraint {
       // ...existing...
       pub user_preconds: Vec<String>,  // raw Cryptol exprs, no llvm_precond wrapper
   }
   ```

   Populate by `user_preconds_map.get(&spec.function_name).cloned().unwrap_or_default()`.
4. In the emitters, after the per-param `llvm_precond` lines and before the `llvm_execute_func` call, emit one `llvm_precond {{ <expr> }};` line per `user_preconds` entry:
   - `src/emit/saw_emit/llvm_spec.rs` for LLVM specs.
   - `src/emit/saw_emit/mir_spec.rs` for MIR specs.
5. The same `user_preconds_map` also unblocks the indirect-enum case at `derive.rs:207-211`. After this lands, update the comment block at L201-L203 to remove the "until that naming is unified" hedge.

## Plumbing notes

- The `--constraints` file path must be resolved relative to the working directory (not the AST/IR path). Use `Path::canonicalize` before reading.
- TOML > JSON because preconditions are multi-line-ish strings and TOML's basic strings are easier to author than JSON-escaped Cryptol.
- For testability, factor the TOML parser into a small `src/constraints/user_preconds.rs` (~80 lines) with its own unit tests over a sample file shipped at `tests/fixtures/user_preconds.toml`.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `saw-spec-gen gen-verify --help` lists `--precond` and `--constraints`.
- [ ] Repeating `--precond fn:expr` two times produces two `llvm_precond` lines on the named function's spec.
- [ ] `--constraints file.toml` is parsed and applied; unknown function names print a stderr warning but exit 0.
- [ ] A new e2e case under `tests/e2e/cases/07-enum-constraints/user_precond/` proves a function that uses `__builtin_unreachable()` on `auth_result > 2`:
  - Without `--precond`: DISPROVED (counterexample `auth_result = 3`).
  - With `--precond AttestKeyOwnership:auth_result <= 2`: VERIFIED.
- [ ] Hand-written MSP `verify_attestation.saw` precondition can be reproduced by regeneration.
- [ ] Source comments at `derive.rs:201-203`, `saw_type.rs:73`, `derive_tests.rs:141` updated to point at the now-real flag (or removed if obsolete).
- [ ] Per-file line counts stay under 500; if `main.rs` or `derive.rs` would exceed, split first.

## Out of scope

- Postcondition support (`llvm_postcond`) — file separately if needed.
- Per-parameter constraint files (the current shape is per-function; per-param is finer-grain but YAGNI for the MSP use case).

title: Wrap emitted prove_print calls with BEGIN_PROOF/PROVED markers (success is silent)
description:
## Problem

SAW's `prove_print` command prints nothing on success — no `Theorem X proved` line, no timing, no acknowledgement. On failure it prints a counterexample, so failure is observable, but success is invisible. For a script that calls `prove_print` 22 times in a row, stdout is empty; the only signal is the SAW process exit code.

This means downstream aggregators cannot:
- Tell which properties were attempted vs. skipped.
- Tell how long each property took.
- Associate a SAW warning/error line with the specific property that triggered it.
- Produce per-property results from a single SAW invocation (blocks option 2 of `saw-spec-gen-0ft`).

## Proposal

When saw-spec-gen emits a Cryptol property script, wrap each `prove_print` with explicit print markers:

```
print "BEGIN_PROOF prop_name";
prove_print z3 {{ prop_name }};
print "PROVED prop_name";
```

On success the script log gets a `PROVED prop_name` line per property. On failure the `BEGIN_PROOF` line tells the aggregator which proof the SAW counterexample below belongs to. Optionally include solver + start/end timestamps so `time_secs` can be computed.

A small `Write-ResultJson`-style PowerShell helper (or the existing `verify*.ps1` flow extended) can then walk the log and emit one `result.json` per property, feeding `collect-results` unchanged.

## Acceptance

- [ ] Generated Cryptol property scripts emit a `BEGIN_PROOF <name>` / `PROVED <name>` marker pair around every `prove_print`.
- [ ] Marker format is documented in [docs/result-json.md](docs/result-json.md) (or a sibling doc) as a stable contract.
- [ ] A PowerShell parser (`scripts/Parse-PropertyLog.ps1` or extension of `Write-ResultJson.ps1`) turns the log into per-property `result.json` files.
- [ ] e2e case asserts the marker lines appear and the resulting manifest has one entry per property.

## Out of scope

Fixing `prove_print` itself in upstream SAW. This is purely a saw-spec-gen emission change.

## Related

- saw-spec-gen-0ft — property-suite scripts have no machine-readable output (this issue unblocks option 2 there).
close_reason: Implemented in feat/proof-markers-dtb: markers emitted on both pipelines, Parse-PropertyLog.ps1 added, e2e case covers parser contract, marker contract documented in docs/proof-markers.md.

title: Generated property-suite scripts have no machine-readable output (only Write-Host)
description:
## Problem

Generated SAW scripts that bundle multiple Cryptol property proofs (e.g. an `verify_properties.saw` that calls `prove_print z3 {{ prop_X }}` for each of N properties) emit nothing machine-readable. Downstream aggregators have to:

1. Read the script source to enumerate the property names.
2. Trust that `exit 0 == all N proven` and `exit non-zero == something failed`.
3. Hand-translate the property list into whatever manifest they need.

Concrete example from MSP: `Run-SAWVerification.ps1` runs `verify_properties.saw` with 22 `prove_print` calls. To build a proof manifest I had to read the .saw, copy the 22 property names by hand, and assume they all passed because the runner exited 0. There is no per-property JSON line, no JUnit, no TAP.

This is a gap relative to the per-function flow, which already emits `result.json` via `scripts/Write-ResultJson.ps1` and aggregates via `cargo run -- collect-results` (see [docs/result-json.md](docs/result-json.md) and [src/collect_results.rs](src/collect_results.rs)). The property-bundle path needs the same contract.

## Proposal

Two options, not mutually exclusive:

1. **One `.saw` per property** — when saw-spec-gen emits Cryptol property scripts, split them so each property gets its own SAW invocation and its own `result.json`. `collect-results` already aggregates these. Pros: zero new schema work. Cons: more SAW startups, slower for large suites.

2. **Multi-result manifest from a single SAW run** — extend the generated SAW property script to print a parseable per-property line (see related issue), and emit a `Write-ResultJson`-style aggregator that walks the SAW stdout and produces one `result.json` per property under `<out>/properties/<prop_name>/result.json`. Then `collect-results` works unchanged.

Either way the acceptance is: after running the generated artifact, `collect-results` produces a `proof_manifest.json` whose `entries[]` has one record per Cryptol property, with no human in the loop.

## Acceptance

- [ ] saw-spec-gen has a documented way to produce machine-readable per-property output for a Cryptol property suite.
- [ ] `collect-results` ingests the output and produces a manifest with one entry per property (status `proven` / `failed`).
- [ ] e2e case under `tests/e2e/cases/` exercising a multi-property `.cry` file end-to-end (saw-spec-gen → SAW → `collect-results` → manifest assertion).
- [ ] [docs/result-json.md](docs/result-json.md) updated to describe how property-bundle results are laid out on disk.

## Related

- saw-spec-gen-xxx (filed alongside): `prove_print` is silent on success, which blocks option 2 above until the emitted script wraps each proof with a marker line.

title: saw-spec-gen: emit input constraints from type system + allow user preconditions
description:
## Problem

Generated SAW specs from `saw-spec-gen` havoc all primitive inputs with no value constraints. For C/C++ code that uses `__builtin_unreachable()` (or Rust code with `unreachable_unchecked!`) on out-of-range enum values, this produces spurious counterexamples: SAW picks an enum tag the source code believes is impossible, the unreachable fires, and verification fails.

Concrete example from MSP: `AttestKeyOwnership` takes an `auth_result` enum with valid values `{0,1,2}`. The default `pretty-specs`-generated spec havocs the full `i32` range, and SAW returns a counterexample with `auth_result = 0xdeadbeef`. The hand-written `verify_attestation.saw` works around this with:

```
llvm_precond {{ auth_result <= 2 }};
```

There is currently no way to express this from `saw-spec-gen`'s CLI or from any input file. Users must hand-edit the generated `.saw` afterward, which defeats the regen-on-change workflow.

## Proposal

Two complementary mechanisms:

1. **Type-system-driven constraints (automatic).** When the AST/MIR shows an `enum`/`enum class` parameter (or a Rust `enum` / `bool`), emit a precondition restricting the symbolic value to the declared variants. For C++ `enum class Foo : uint8_t { A=0, B=1, C=2 }`, emit `llvm_precond {{ foo <= 2 }}` (or an explicit set membership for non-contiguous tags). For `bool`, emit `<= 1`.
   - Source: `parsers/clang_ast/enum_bits.rs` already extracts enum width and (presumably) variant set.
   - For Rust, `parsers/mir_json/adt.rs` has variant info.

2. **User-supplied constraints (manual override).** Add a CLI flag and/or per-function constraint file so users can attach extra `llvm_precond` / `mir_precond` expressions without editing the generated spec. Suggested shapes:
   - `--precond "fn_name:auth_result <= 2"` (repeatable), or
   - `--constraints constraints.toml` with `[fn_name] preconds = ["auth_result <= 2", "len > 0"]`.
   - Emit these into the generated spec immediately after the havoc block, before the call.

The `constraints/` module is the natural home for the type-driven version; emission goes through `emit/saw_emit/llvm_spec.rs` and `mir_spec.rs`.

## Acceptance

- [ ] Enum / bool parameters automatically get range preconditions in generated `.saw`.
- [ ] CLI flag or constraint file lets users add arbitrary Cryptol preconditions per function.
- [ ] New e2e case under `tests/e2e/cases/` covering a function that uses `__builtin_unreachable()` on an out-of-range enum:
  - Without the feature: verification yields DISPROVED with a counterexample outside the enum range.
  - With the feature: verification yields VERIFIED.
  - Entry added to `tests/e2e/cases.psd1` with expected verdict `VERIFIED`.
- [ ] Hand-written MSP `verify_attestation.saw` precondition can be reproduced by regeneration.

## Related / out of scope here

- `pretty-specs` CLI only accepts one `.cry` file at a time — no `--specs-dir` or glob. MSP needed a shell loop over 6 spec files. Filing separately.
notes:
2026-06-02 status check (Claude): the "Type-system-driven constraints (automatic)" half of the proposal is already implemented and shipped.

Wired pieces:
- `src/constraints/value_clauses.rs` dispatches per-TypeInfo and emits Cryptol clauses (`{name} <= ({max} : [{bits}])`) for `Enum`, `<= 1` for `Option` and `Result`, and recurses through `Struct` fields. Scalar types (`Bool`/`SignedInt`/`UnsignedInt`/`Float`/`Pointer`/`ByteArray`/`Opaque`/`Void`) intentionally emit no clause.
- `src/constraints/derive.rs` lines 207-211 wrap each clause in `llvm_precond {{ ... }}` and pushes it into `ParamSpec.preconditions`, gated on `AllocType::FreshVar` (by-value primitives only).
- Tests: `test_derive_enum_discriminant_constraint`, `test_derive_enum_indirect_skips_precondition`, `test_derive_return_enum_constraint` in `src/constraints/derive_tests.rs`, plus the per-clause tests in `src/constraints/value_clauses.rs`.
- End-to-end coverage: `tests/e2e/cases/07-enum-constraints/`.

Remaining work has been split out:
- saw-spec-gen-xup (new): wire `--precond` / `--constraints` CLI flag. The string `--precond` already appears in source comments (`derive.rs:201-203`, `saw_type.rs:73`, `derive_tests.rs:141`) referring to a flag that was never actually added to `src/main.rs`. Until it lands, indirect-passed (pointer) enums get no automatic clause and there is no user override path.
- saw-spec-gen-xgp (new): enums with non-contiguous explicit discriminants are over-constrained. `value_clauses.rs:57-67` emits `<= variants.len() - 1`, which is wrong for `enum E : u8 { A=0, B=2, C=100 }`.

Recommend closing this umbrella once both follow-ups merge.

title: Restore unknown_impl &dyn Trait e2e test with real tool support
description: Removed the trait_unknown_impl e2e case (tests/e2e/cases/03-rust-trait-dispatch/unknown_impl/) on 2026-05-29 because it relied on a hand-rolled SAW driver (run_unknown_impl.ps1) and a duplicated trait_schema.json that re-stated the trait definition already present in the .rs source. That gave false confidence: the verifier path being exercised was bespoke, not the production verify-rust.ps1 path. Restore once saw-spec-gen can drive the full flow end-to-end from .rs source alone.
design: Add src/parsers/rust_source.rs (lightweight regex-based parser, scoped to pub trait + pub fn forms; reject unsupported types) producing TraitSchema and FnSig. Extend gen-rust-trait-stubs with --rust-file (mutually exclusive with --schema). Teach verify-rust.ps1 a dyn-trait branch that relaxes the integer-only signature check, calls llvm-as on the generated stubs.ll, llvm-link's the bitcode, and emits a driver using llvm_global '__stubvtable_<Trait>' for the vtable arg.
acceptance_criteria: 1. saw-spec-gen gen-rust-trait-stubs accepts --rust-file <foo.rs> and derives the TraitSchema in-process (no JSON intermediate, no hand-written schema). 2. verify-rust.ps1 detects '&dyn TraitName' parameters in the target fn signature, invokes the stub generator, links the produced .ll into the bitcode, and emits a SAW driver that allocates fat-pointer args (data ptr + vtable global) and passes trait_overrides to llvm_verify. 3. The two add_step cases (verified -> VERIFIED, disproved -> DISPROVED) round-trip via Runner='rust' in cases.psd1 -- no custom Script entry. 4. trait_schema.json and run_unknown_impl.ps1 are not reintroduced.

title: Restore async Rust e2e test once verify-rust.ps1 supports coroutine resume functions
description: The async_rust e2e case (formerly tests/e2e/cases/06-async-rust/add_one_coroutine/) was removed in commit <ADDED-IN-COMMIT> because run_async_test.ps1 was a custom runner whose existence hid a real tool gap: verify-rust.ps1 only supports plain (iN, ...) -> iN signatures, so async fn add_one(x: u32) -> u32 (which rustc lowers to (sret([12 x i8]), i32) -> void with the actual arithmetic in a separately-emitted coroutine resume symbol) cannot be verified through the standard pipeline.
design: Pieces the deleted runner had to know that the standard wrapper does not: (1) coroutine state layout — for add_one(x: u32) it is { i32 x; i32 polled; i8 tag } (12 bytes, align 4); (2) symbol mangling for the resume function — _RNCNvCs<hash>_<crate>7add_one0<...>; (3) Poll<u32> return ABI — { i32 disc, i32 val } where Ready = (0, v); (4) opaque task context allocation. Steps 1 and 3 generalise from rustc's coroutine lowering pass; step 2 is a regex sweep over llvm-dis output. None of this is async-specific to add_one — it should live in saw-spec-gen so any async fn with integer args/return Just Works.
acceptance_criteria: verify-rust.ps1 (or a dedicated subcommand) detects async fn / coroutine lowering, resolves the resume symbol, sets up the coroutine state struct, and verifies it against the Cryptol spec WITHOUT a per-test bespoke .ps1 driver. Then restore the test under tests/e2e/cases/06-async-rust/ with Runner='rust' in cases.psd1.

title: Document and version-stamp result.json schema
description:
The per-run `result.json` file written by verify.ps1 / verify-rust.ps1 / verify-equiv.ps1 is currently defined inline in each wrapper (verify.ps1:283-296, verify-rust.ps1:395-408) and read by verify-equiv.ps1:110-118 with no schema document. The pretty-specs adapter (saw-spec-gen-3eq + pretty-specs-2oa) needs a stable contract.

Work:
- Add `schema_version: "1"` field to every result.json payload.
- Document the schema in tests/e2e/README.md (or a new `docs/result-json.md`): fields `side` (cpp|rust|equiv), `function`, `cryptol_fn`, `verdict` (VERIFIED|DISPROVED|UNKNOWN|EQUIVALENT|NOT EQUIVALENT), `counterexample` (array of {name, value, bits}), `expected`, `actual`, plus optional `solver`, `time_secs`, `impl_file`.
- Factor the writer into a single PowerShell helper module (e.g. scripts/Write-ResultJson.ps1) sourced by all three verify scripts so the shape stays in sync.
- Optional: emit a JSON Schema (.beads/schemas/result.v1.json or similar) and add a tiny test that validates one fixture.

Acceptance:
- All three verify scripts produce result.json files with a `schema_version` field.
- Schema doc exists and is linked from README.md.
- The collect-results subcommand (saw-spec-gen-3eq) rejects unknown schema versions with a clear error.

title: Add collect-results subcommand: aggregate result.json into pretty-specs proof manifest
description:
Add a collect-results subcommand to saw-spec-gen that walks a directory tree, finds every out_*/result.json produced by verify.ps1 / verify-rust.ps1 / verify-equiv.ps1, and emits a single proof_manifest.json in the format pretty-specs (../pretty-specs) consumes via its --proof-status <FILE> flag.

This is the saw-spec-gen-side half of the closed-loop "verified badges in docs" integration. Pairs with pretty-specs-2oa (which proposes the same adapter — by putting it here we avoid coupling pretty-specs to our result.json shape).

Inputs:
- `--root <DIR>` (required): directory to scan recursively for `result.json`
- `--output <FILE>` (default `proof_manifest.json`): manifest path
- `--cryptol-fn-map <FILE>` (optional): JSON map of impl-fn -> Cryptol-fn aliases when names differ
- `--format flat|structured` (default `flat` until pretty-specs-ua2 lands)

Behavior:
- Skip `out_*` dirs with no `result.json` (verification didn't run yet).
- Use the verdict mapping: VERIFIED -> proven; DISPROVED -> failed (reason from counterexample); UNKNOWN -> not_attempted; EQUIVALENT/NOT EQUIVALENT -> proven/failed with source=equiv.
- Key entries by `function` field from result.json. When --cryptol-fn-map applies, key by Cryptol-fn instead so pretty-specs matches by Item::Function.name.

Acceptance:
- `saw-spec-gen collect-results --root tests/e2e/cases --output manifest.json` produces a manifest covering every case the e2e suite ran.
- Round-trip: feed manifest to pretty-specs and the function pages render badges (once pretty-specs-8yu + pretty-specs-354 land).
- Unit test against fixture result.json files.

title: OOM on large AST files -- need streaming parser or helpful error
description: Large clang AST dumps (100MB+) cause OOM when read_to_string + serde_json::from_str loads the entire file. Either implement a streaming parser that processes nodes incrementally, or at minimum detect file size and emit a helpful error with suggestions to use -ast-dump-filter or split the input.
close_reason: Added file size guards (100MB for AST/MIR, 200MB for LLVM IR) in parse_ast, parse_mir, and parse_llvm_ir. On oversized files, emits actionable error with suggestions (use -ast-dump-filter, opt --passes=internalize, jq pre-filtering). 3 new tests.

title: Handle concatenated JSON objects from clang -ast-dump-filter
description: clang -ast-dump=json -ast-dump-filter=FuncName emits multiple top-level JSON objects concatenated (not a JSON array). serde_json::from_str fails on this. Need to detect and handle multiple concatenated JSON objects by splitting on top-level braces or using a streaming deserializer.
close_reason: parse_ast now handles concatenated JSON objects from clang -ast-dump-filter. Falls back to brace-depth splitting when standard parse fails. Wraps multiple objects in a synthetic TranslationUnitDecl. String-aware splitter handles nested braces in JSON values. 5 new tests.

title: Model sret (struct return) calling convention for functions returning large types
description: When LLVM lowers a function returning a large struct, it transforms the return into an sret pointer parameter. The from-llvm-ir parser needs to detect sret attributes and model the function as taking an output pointer parameter (AllocMutable) instead of having a return value.
close_reason: Implemented sret detection: finds sret attribute on parameters, models as writeonly output pointer, sets return type to void. Handles nested parens in sret(type). 3 new tests.

title: Resolve C++ class/STL type sizes instead of emitting zero-size opaque types
description: When parse_cpp_type encounters unknown types like std::string, std::vector, or user classes not in struct_map, it emits Opaque { size_bytes: 0 }. Add a well-known STL type size table for MSVC x64 (std::string=32, std::vector=24, etc.) and also compute sizes from struct field layouts.
close_reason: Added STL type size table (MSVC x64 ABI) covering string, vector, map, shared_ptr, unique_ptr, mutex, function, span, string_view, plus Win32 types (GUID, CRITICAL_SECTION, etc). Also computes struct sizes from field layouts with proper alignment padding. 9 new tests.

title: Implement from-llvm-ir subcommand for rich type layout info
description: Parse struct type definitions (%struct.Name = type { ... }) from LLVM IR .ll files, compute their sizes, and produce TypeInfo::Struct with fields and size_bytes instead of zero-size Opaque types. This lets generated SAW specs use llvm_array N (llvm_int 8) for proper allocation sizes.
close_reason: Implemented struct type definition parsing from LLVM IR. Computes sizes with padding and alignment for normal and packed structs. 7 new tests.

title: Generate Cryptol type constraint functions alongside SAW specs
description: In addition to .saw files, optionally generate .cry (Cryptol) files with type constraint functions. E.g., for Result<T,E> generate a Cryptol function that constrains discriminant to {0,1}. These can be imported into existing Cryptol specs to add type-system-derived bounds. Add --emit-cryptol flag.

title: MIR JSON: handle async function coroutine types
description: Async Rust functions in mir-json produce TyCoroutine types with discriminant states and upvar fields. Generate mir_coroutine_value-based specs that constrain the coroutine state to valid discriminant values and properly type the captured variables. Needed for verifying async KeyExchangeSession methods.

title: Implement from-llvm-ir subcommand for language-agnostic attribute extraction
description: Parse LLVM IR text (.ll files from llvm-dis) to extract function attributes: readonly, writeonly, noalias, nocapture, nounwind, nonnull, dereferenceable(N), noundef, align(N), range. This is language-agnostic and works for any language compiled to LLVM (C, C++, Rust, Swift, etc.). Less rich than clang AST or MIR but requires no source-level tooling.

title: Handle noexcept and exception specifications
description: C++ noexcept functions cannot throw. The clang AST encodes this in the function type (noexcept qualifier). Generate can_throw=false for noexcept functions. For non-noexcept functions, emit a comment warning that exception paths are possible. Future: integrate with SAW exception-lowering pass to generate specs that model both success and throw paths.

title: Handle C++ template/generic functions in clang AST
description: clang AST includes FunctionTemplateDecl for template functions. These have type parameters (T, U) that need to be resolved or flagged as unresolvable. For MSP C++ this matters for std:: functions like std::find, std::transform. Generate specs that are parameterized or flag as needing manual instantiation.

title: pretty-specs: accept multiple .cry files or --specs-dir
description:
pretty-specs currently accepts a single .cry file per invocation. In the MSP repo we had to shell-loop over 6 spec files. Add either:

- positional varargs: `pretty-specs spec1.cry spec2.cry ...`, or
- `--specs-dir <dir>` that picks up every `*.cry` underneath, or
- both.

Mentioned as a sibling concern in saw-spec-gen-xip but tracked separately because it's an independent CLI change.

title: Add -Help / usage output to verify*.ps1 scripts
description:
The verify scripts (verify.ps1, verify-rust.ps1, verify-equiv.ps1, verify-all.ps1) currently have no built-in help. Users have to read the script source to discover parameters, expected inputs, and exit codes.

Add a comment-based help block (.SYNOPSIS / .DESCRIPTION / .PARAMETER / .EXAMPLE) to each verify script so 'Get-Help ./verify.ps1' and './verify.ps1 -?' produce useful output. Also document the RESULT: line contract that Run-E2ETests.ps1 parses.

title: Emit type metadata JSON to support pretty-specs type cross-check
Batch verify mode: consume pretty-specs --emit-function-list output
description:
Today every verify run requires explicit `--cryptol-fn` and `--function` pairs. With pretty-specs-k1g (`--emit-function-list`), pretty-specs will emit a JSON array of {name, signature, arity} for every Item::Function in a parsed .cry spec. saw-spec-gen should be able to consume that and iterate.

Add either:
- A new `--spec-manifest <FILE>` flag on `gen-verify` that reads the function list and runs gen-verify once per function, OR
- A wrapper subcommand `verify-all --spec-manifest <FILE> --cpp <DIR> --rust <DIR>` that orchestrates verify.ps1 / verify-rust.ps1 invocations and writes a consolidated output dir.

The wrapper approach is preferred because it composes cleanly with collect-results (saw-spec-gen-3eq) for the full pipeline:

  pretty-specs SDEP.cry --emit-function-list > fns.json
  saw-spec-gen verify-all --spec-manifest fns.json --cpp impl/ --output runs/
  saw-spec-gen collect-results --root runs/ --output manifest.json
  pretty-specs SDEP.cry -o docs/ --proof-status manifest.json

Acceptance:
- One command discovers every Item::Function in a .cry spec and attempts verification for each.
- Functions with no implementation get logged but don't fail the run.
- Output dir is laid out so collect-results can find each run's result.json.
